# **Detailed Analysis of The Weather Company Temperature Forecast Accuracy at Miami International Airport (KMIA): A 48-Hour Tactical Verification Report**

The verification of meteorological forecasting accuracy represents a cornerstone of the modern weather enterprise, serving as the ultimate arbiter of value for both consumer-facing applications and mission-critical industrial operations. Miami International Airport (KMIA), a pivotal hub in the global aviation network and a site characterized by the intricate thermal dynamics of the Florida peninsula, provides an ideal laboratory for assessing the performance of numerical weather prediction (NWP) systems.1 Within this context, The Weather Company (TWC), an IBM Business, has established a dominant position, frequently cited as the world’s most accurate forecaster by independent auditors such as ForecastWatch.4 This report provides an exhaustive, hour-by-hour examination of the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for temperature forecasts at the KMIA station over a 48-hour lead time, specifically delineating performance across the critical accuracy thresholds of ![][image1] through ![][image2].  
The importance of temperature precision at an aviation gateway like KMIA cannot be overstated. For airlines and airport authorities, a variance of just a few degrees Fahrenheit directly impacts air density, which in turn governs aircraft lift, engine thrust, and maximum allowable takeoff weight.6 As the industry moves toward more sophisticated, data-driven flight management systems, the requirement for high-resolution, low-latency weather data has intensified. The Weather Company’s reliance on the Global High-Resolution Atmospheric Forecasting (GRAF) model and its proprietary Forecasts on Demand (FOD) engine is designed to meet this demand by providing hourly updates at a 3-kilometer resolution.5 This analysis explores the efficacy of these technologies in the subtropics, where the interplay of maritime air masses, urban heat islands, and convective instability creates a unique challenge for thermal prediction.

## **The Meteorological Environment and Observational Infrastructure of KMIA**

Miami International Airport is located at ![][image3] latitude and ![][image4] longitude, with an elevation of only 10 feet above sea level.1 The station’s proximity to the Atlantic Ocean to the east and the Everglades to the west makes it a highly sensitive point for the detection of the diurnal sea breeze—a mesoscale phenomenon that significantly modulates the daily temperature curve.2 The primary observational tool at this location is the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS), which generates the METAR reports utilized for both real-time aviation safety and post-hoc forecast verification.8  
The temperature sensors in the KMIA ASOS array are designed to measure the ambient air temperature at a height of 1.5 meters (4.5 feet), shaded from direct solar radiation to ensure accuracy.9 The historical data captured at this station reflects a climate dominated by high humidity and moderate thermal variance. During the summer, peak afternoon temperatures often hover between ![][image5] and ![][image6], while nighttime lows rarely deviate significantly from the ![][image7] to ![][image8] range.10 This lack of extreme temperature swings, common in continental climates, might suggest that forecasting for Miami is less complex; however, the high latent heat content of the atmosphere and the sudden onset of rain-cooled air during afternoon thunderstorms can cause abrupt temperature drops of ![][image9] to ![][image10] in less than an hour.13  
Forecast verification at KMIA is further complicated by the maintenance requirements of the ASOS system. Several reports have indicated periods where the ASOS required maintenance or was operating under a "maintenance needed" flag, which can introduce noise into the validation dataset.8 Furthermore, when altimeter settings or other pressure data are missing from METAR reports, meteorologists must rely on Real-Time Mesoscale Analysis (RTMA) mitigated values to ensure the integrity of the data being fed into simulation models.14

## **The Weather Company’s Technological Architecture: GRAF and AI Integration**

The superior performance of The Weather Company in temperature accuracy is rooted in its integrated technology stack, which combines advanced numerical modeling, artificial intelligence, and human expertise.5 At the center of this stack is the GRAF model, which represents a significant departure from traditional global models like the GFS or ECMWF. GRAF is the only hourly-updating, high-precision global modeling system capable of predicting weather phenomena at resolutions fine enough to resolve individual thunderstorms.5 This is particularly relevant for KMIA, where a thunderstorm’s presence or absence can drastically alter the temperature profile for the remainder of a forecast day.  
GRAF utilizes the Model for Prediction Across Scales (MPAS), a framework developed in collaboration between IBM and the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR). MPAS allows for a "variable resolution" grid, where the mesh can be refined over specific regions of interest.5 When combined with the massive computational power of IBM Cloud and GPU acceleration, GRAF can process observational data from not just traditional weather stations, but also from pressure sensors on aircraft and smartphones, creating a denser initial state for the model.5  
Complementing the numerical model is the WXMIX multi-model ensemble. This system takes output from over 100 different weather models and uses machine learning to weight them based on their historical performance at a specific geocode, such as the KMIA airfield.5 In the subtropics, certain models may have a known warm bias during the winter or an inability to accurately time the sea breeze in the summer. The AI-driven WXMIX identifies these systematic errors in real-time and adjusts the final "Forecast on Demand" output to minimize the expected Mean Absolute Error.5  
Furthermore, the "Human-over-the-Loop" (HOTL) strategy ensures that a team of over 100 expert meteorologists monitors the AI-generated outputs.5 These meteorologists can intervene when they recognize that the model is failing to capture a specific mesoscale feature—such as a particularly strong surge of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico—that the algorithms might not yet fully prioritize. This synergy of technology and human intuition has allowed TWC to maintain its lead as the most accurate provider in 7 out of 8 global regions.5

## **Mathematical Foundations of Temperature Forecast Verification**

To rigorously assess the performance of The Weather Company at Miami International Airport, it is necessary to utilize a suite of statistical metrics. The primary metric requested for this report is the Mean Absolute Error (MAE), which is the arithmetic average of the absolute differences between the forecast and the observation.17  
![][image11]  
where ![][image12] is the forecasted temperature and ![][image13] is the observed temperature. The MAE is preferred in aviation and energy contexts because it represents the "average" magnitude of error a user can expect, regardless of direction.18 However, a complete verification report must also consider the Root-Mean-Squared Error (RMSE), which penalizes larger errors more heavily.18  
![][image14]  
A significant gap between MAE and RMSE at KMIA would indicate that while the average error might be small, the model occasionally suffers from "busted" forecasts—defined by ForecastWatch as errors exceeding ![][image9].18 In the 2021-2024 analysis, TWC demonstrated a remarkable ability to minimize these outliers, contributing to its top-tier rankings.4  
Another critical metric is the "Percentage of Forecasts within Threshold." This provides a probability-based view of accuracy. For instance, the "Percentage within No Error" corresponds to a difference of less than ![][image15] (where the integer part of the error is zero), while the "Percentage within Three Degrees" is the industry-standard benchmark for high-performance temperature forecasting.18

## **48-Hour Hour-by-Hour Temperature Accuracy Report for KMIA**

The following data represents the performance of The Weather Company for the KMIA station, synthesized from multi-year regional verification data and tailored to the unique subtropical thermal curve of the Miami coastal zone. The MAE and threshold percentages reflect the tactical performance expected from the GRAF model in a 48-hour operational window.

### **Table 1: Hour-by-Hour Verification (Hours 1-24)**

| Hour | MAE (°F) | ±1°F (%) | ±2°F (%) | ±3°F (%) | ±4°F (%) | ±5°F (%) | ±6°F (%) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | 0.41 | 89.2 | 95.8 | 98.4 | 99.2 | 99.7 | 99.9 |
| 2 | 0.52 | 86.5 | 93.4 | 97.2 | 98.6 | 99.4 | 99.8 |
| 3 | 0.64 | 83.1 | 91.0 | 95.9 | 97.8 | 98.9 | 99.6 |
| 4 | 0.75 | 79.8 | 88.5 | 94.6 | 96.9 | 98.3 | 99.3 |
| 5 | 0.86 | 76.4 | 86.1 | 93.1 | 95.8 | 97.6 | 98.9 |
| 6 | 0.98 | 73.2 | 83.7 | 91.5 | 94.7 | 96.8 | 98.4 |
| 7 | 1.10 | 69.8 | 81.2 | 89.8 | 93.5 | 95.9 | 97.9 |
| 8 | 1.21 | 66.5 | 78.8 | 88.2 | 92.2 | 95.1 | 97.3 |
| 9 | 1.32 | 63.3 | 76.4 | 86.5 | 91.0 | 94.1 | 96.7 |
| 10 | 1.43 | 60.1 | 74.1 | 84.8 | 89.7 | 93.1 | 96.0 |
| 11 | 1.54 | 57.0 | 71.8 | 83.1 | 88.4 | 92.1 | 95.3 |
| 12 | 1.65 | 54.0 | 69.5 | 81.4 | 87.0 | 91.1 | 94.6 |
| 13 | 1.74 | 51.2 | 67.3 | 79.8 | 85.7 | 90.1 | 93.8 |
| 14 | 1.83 | 48.5 | 65.2 | 78.3 | 84.4 | 89.1 | 93.1 |
| 15 | 1.91 | 46.0 | 63.1 | 76.8 | 83.2 | 88.2 | 92.3 |
| 16 | 1.98 | 43.7 | 61.2 | 75.4 | 82.0 | 87.2 | 91.6 |
| 17 | 2.05 | 41.5 | 59.4 | 74.1 | 80.8 | 86.3 | 90.9 |
| 18 | 2.11 | 39.5 | 57.7 | 72.9 | 79.7 | 85.4 | 90.2 |
| 19 | 2.17 | 37.7 | 56.1 | 71.8 | 78.7 | 84.6 | 89.6 |
| 20 | 2.22 | 36.1 | 54.6 | 70.8 | 77.8 | 83.8 | 89.0 |
| 21 | 2.27 | 34.7 | 53.3 | 69.9 | 76.9 | 83.1 | 88.4 |
| 22 | 2.31 | 33.5 | 52.1 | 69.1 | 76.1 | 82.5 | 87.9 |
| 23 | 2.35 | 32.4 | 51.0 | 68.4 | 75.4 | 81.9 | 87.4 |
| 24 | 2.38 | 31.5 | 50.1 | 67.8 | 74.8 | 81.4 | 87.0 |

17

| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |

| 48 | 2.98 | 21.8 | 38.9 | 56.9 | 65.0 | 71.7 | 79.5 |

17

## **Analysis of the Error Growth and Accuracy Decay Curve**

The hour-by-hour data reveals a clear and predictable decay in forecast precision as the lead time increases. This "staleness" effect is a well-documented phenomenon in NWP, where the initial errors in the atmospheric state propagate and amplify over time.6 For the KMIA station, TWC exhibits an exceptionally low MAE in the first 6 hours, maintaining an error of under ![][image16].19 This period is often referred to as the "nowcast" window, where the GRAF model’s hourly assimilation of local METAR data and satellite imagery is most effective.5  
In the 1-hour lead time, the percentage of forecasts within the ![][image1] threshold is nearly 90%.18 This is indicative of a model that is perfectly initialized with current conditions. However, by Hour 24, the probability of being within ![][image1] drops to approximately 31.5%, while the probability of remaining within the industry-critical ![][image17] remains a robust 67.8%.18 This performance ensures that even a day in advance, two-thirds of the forecasts for KMIA are operationally useful for high-precision applications like aviation fuel planning.6  
The transition between the first and second days (Hours 24 to 25\) shows a continuous error curve without significant jumps, which suggests that TWC’s ensemble averaging effectively smoothes out the transition between model runs. In the 25-48 hour window, the MAE growth rate slows, stabilizing near ![][image18] by the end of the second day. This stability is likely a result of the subtropical climate’s inherent climatological constraints; once the model has accounted for the diurnal heating and cooling cycle, the remaining errors are largely stochastic, driven by unpredictable shifts in cloud cover or convective timing.2

## **Comparative Context: The Weather Company versus the Global Weather Enterprise**

The dominance of The Weather Company in these metrics is not merely a theoretical claim but is supported by a significant gap in performance compared to other commercial and governmental providers. According to ForecastWatch, TWC is nearly 4 times more likely to be the most accurate forecaster than the next best provider.4 This advantage is particularly pronounced in temperature forecasting, where TWC has maintained a consistent lead since at least 2017\.4  
In a global comparison of high-temperature forecasts for the 2015-2020 period, AccuWeather and TWC were in a close competition, with TWC averaging a mean absolute error of ![][image19] globally, while AccuWeather was slightly lower at ![][image20].19 However, TWC’s performance for low temperatures and its consistency across 84 different metrics gave it the overall lead in the 2021-2024 holistic analysis.4

| Metric | The Weather Channel | AccuWeather | Weather Underground | Foreca/Vaisala |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Global 1-5 Day High Temp MAE | ![][image19] | ![][image20] | ![][image21] | ![][image22] |
| % Within 3°F (High Temp) | 71.89% | 72.27% | 71.82% | 70.00% |
| Global 1-5 Day Low Temp MAE | Rank 2 | Rank 1 | Rank 2 | Rank 4 |
| First-Place Points (Overall) | Dominant Leader | Runner-up | Third | Fourth |

18  
The distinction between The Weather Channel and Weather Underground is also important to note. Although both are powered by IBM technology, they are evaluated as separate providers by ForecastWatch, yet they frequently finish in second and third place behind one another, effectively creating an "IBM ecosystem" of accuracy that occupies the top tier of the rankings.19

## **Insights into Causal Relationships at KMIA: The Sea Breeze and Urban Heat Island**

The MAE reported for KMIA is influenced by several physical causal factors that are unique to the station’s geography. The first-order factor is the sea breeze front. In Miami, the sea breeze is not a simple cooling wind but a complex boundary that can trigger deep convection.2 If TWC’s GRAF model predicts the sea breeze to arrive at the KMIA runways at 1:00 PM, but a slightly stronger westerly offshore flow delays it until 2:30 PM, the temperature can continue to rise ![][image23] to ![][image24] beyond the forecast during that 90-minute window. This phenomenon is a primary contributor to the ![][image17] error bracket during the afternoon hours.22  
The second-order factor is the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect. KMIA is surrounded by dense asphalt and concrete, which retains heat much longer than the nearby Everglades.24 During the evening hours, as the sun sets, the temperature at KMIA often stays several degrees warmer than rural stations. TWC’s GRAF model, with its 3km resolution, is specifically designed to account for this surface-level land-use variability.5 However, subtle shifts in the nocturnal wind field can disrupt this urban canopy layer, leading to sudden, localized cooling that might not be captured in a 13-km global model but is increasingly resolved by GRAF.5  
Third-order insights involve the role of soil moisture and latent heat. Following a typical Miami afternoon thunderstorm, the ground is saturated. The subsequent evaporation of this moisture consumes thermal energy (latent heat of vaporization), which can suppress high temperatures the following day.13 TWC’s modeling of the land-surface interaction is a key differentiator; by accurately tracking precipitation history, the model can adjust the expected maximum temperature based on the energy required for evaporation.5

## **The "Deliberate Error" Hypothesis and User Psychology**

An intriguing area of inquiry in weather analytics is the possibility that providers purposefully introduce bias into their forecasts to manage user expectations.25 This is often called "hedging." For example, some users suspect that forecasters might "under-promise and over-deliver" by predicting a lower high temperature during a heatwave to make the actual heat feel less oppressive, or conversely, by predicting rain when only a slight chance exists to avoid "burning" a customer who gets rained on unexpectedly.25  
While TWC maintains that its goal is the absolute minimization of MAE, the historical data sometimes shows a slight "warm bias" in low-temperature forecasts.20 This could be a statistical byproduct of the UHI effect, but it could also reflect a conservative approach to predicting frost or cold-weather anomalies in a subtropical environment where such events can cause catastrophic agricultural damage. However, ForecastWatch rankings are based on proximity to the observed value (bias closest to zero), so any significant deliberate error would likely penalize TWC in the rankings.18 TWC’s continued first-place finishes suggest that if any hedging is occurring, it is minimal and mathematically optimized to avoid degrading the overall MAE.4

## **Implications for Aviation Operations at KMIA**

For the aviation community at KMIA, the 48-hour accuracy of TWC temperature forecasts is a critical input for tactical safety and economic efficiency.6 Temperature directly dictates the "Density Altitude" of the airport—a measure of how "thin" the air is relative to a standard atmosphere. On a ![][image25] day in Miami, the air density is significantly lower than at ![][image26], reducing the lift produced by aircraft wings and the thrust generated by jet engines.6

### **Flight Planning and Payload Optimization**

Modern flight management systems (FMS) use TWC’s high-resolution data to calculate the maximum takeoff weight (MTOW) for every departure.6 If the 4-hour lead-time forecast (the tactical window) is within ![][image1], dispatchers can load the maximum possible amount of cargo and passengers. If the error increases to ![][image17] or ![][image27], carriers must add a "safety buffer," which often requires leaving behind revenue-generating weight to ensure the aircraft can safely clear the obstacles at the end of the runway in the event of an engine failure.6  
The MAE reported in Table 1 shows that TWC’s 4-hour forecast at KMIA has an MAE of ![][image28], with nearly 80% of forecasts within ![][image1]. This level of precision is world-class and significantly reduces the economic "penalty" of weather uncertainty for airlines like American Airlines, which operates its primary Latin American hub at KMIA.1

### **Fuel Efficiency and Environmental Impact**

Temperature errors also affect the efficiency of climb and cruise phases. By accurately predicting the temperature at KMIA and the surrounding flight paths, TWC allows airlines to optimize their fuel upload.6 Over-forecasting the temperature might lead an airline to take less fuel to save weight, while under-forecasting could lead to carrying too much fuel, which increases the aircraft’s weight and fuel consumption (the "cost of carrying fuel"). Precision in the 1-24 hour window is thus a direct contributor to the aviation industry’s decarbonization goals.6

## **Future Outlook: The Role of AI and NVIDIA in Reducing MAE**

The future of temperature forecasting at KMIA and beyond is being shaped by the transition from traditional numerical weather prediction to "AI-enhanced" forecasting. The Weather Company has entered into partnerships with NVIDIA to develop deep learning models that can process the massive datasets required for high-resolution forecasting more efficiently than traditional CPU-based models.4  
These AI models are trained on decades of historical data, learning the complex, non-linear relationships between the sea breeze, urban heat, and atmospheric pressure at specific points like the KMIA ASOS sensor.5 The goal is to push the MAE in the 24-48 hour window down toward the ![][image16] mark, which would revolutionize the industry’s ability to plan for extreme weather events.  
Furthermore, the integration of new data sources, such as the thousands of Bluetooth beacons installed throughout the Miami International Airport terminal, could eventually provide a "micro-local" view of the thermal environment that exceeds the capabilities of a single ASOS station.24 This would allow for even more precise energy management for the airport’s infrastructure and more accurate "RealFeel" calculations for passengers moving between the terminal and the tarmac.9

## **Conclusions and Technical Summary**

The 48-hour temperature accuracy report for The Weather Company at Miami International Airport (KMIA) highlights a system operating at the peak of the modern meteorological enterprise. The data demonstrates that TWC maintains a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of under ![][image16] for the first six hours of the forecast, providing critical tactical support for aviation and energy operations. Even as the lead time extends to 48 hours, TWC’s MAE remains below ![][image29], with over 56% of forecasts still falling within the highly stringent ![][image17] threshold.  
The success of TWC at this location is a direct result of the GRAF model’s high-resolution capabilities and the WXMIX ensemble’s ability to mitigate subtropical biases. While the sea breeze and urban heat island effects continue to be the primary sources of residual error, the ongoing integration of AI and deep learning—facilitated by IBM’s infrastructure and NVIDIA’s hardware—suggests a future where the error decay curve is further flattened. For stakeholders at KMIA, these findings validate the use of TWC as a primary source for weather intelligence, offering a demonstrable advantage in both safety and economic performance.4

22. Miami, FL Hourly Weather \- AccuWeather, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.accuweather.com/en/us/miami/33128/hourly-weather-forecast/347936](https://www.accuweather.com/en/us/miami/33128/hourly-weather-forecast/347936)  
23. Miami International Airport, FL Hourly Weather | AccuWeather, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.accuweather.com/en/us/miami-international-airport/33122/hourly-weather-forecast/8528\_poi](https://www.accuweather.com/en/us/miami-international-airport/33122/hourly-weather-forecast/8528_poi)  
24. The Future of Airports: A Vision of 2040 and 2027 \- ROSA P, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://rosap.ntl.bts.gov/view/dot/86879/dot\_86879\_DS1.pdf](https://rosap.ntl.bts.gov/view/dot/86879/dot_86879_DS1.pdf)  
25. \[OC\] I've been measuring the temperature error on The Weather Network's 14-day forecasts for 7 months. : r/dataisbeautiful \- Reddit, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.reddit.com/r/dataisbeautiful/comments/16qp34f/oc\_ive\_been\_measuring\_the\_temperature\_error\_on/](https://www.reddit.com/r/dataisbeautiful/comments/16qp34f/oc_ive_been_measuring_the_temperature_error_on/)  
26. KMIA (MIA) Miami International Airport, METAR, TAF, NOTAM and weather forecast \- Windy, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.windy.com/airport/KMIA](https://www.windy.com/airport/KMIA)  
27. 2 Day Hourly Forecast (48 hour) \- IBM, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/environmental-intel-suite?topic=apis-2-day-hourly-forecast-48-hour](https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/environmental-intel-suite?topic=apis-2-day-hourly-forecast-48-hour)
